title: Supporting the goals and ideals of International Mother Language Day in bringing attention to the importance of preserving linguistic and cultural heritage through education.
plain_summary: This bill supports International Mother Language Day, which happens every February 21st. The day is meant to help people understand why it's important to protect and save different languages from around the world. The House of Representatives wants Americans to join in special events and activities on this day. They believe it's valuable for people to speak different languages and keep their cultural traditions alive.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Oversight and Government Reform.

sections.title: Resolution Title
sections.text: This resolution supports the goals and ideals of International Mother Language Day in bringing attention to the importance of preserving linguistic and cultural heritage through education.

sections.title: Background Facts
sections.text: The United Nations Educational, Scientific, and Cultural Organization (UNESCO) created International Mother Language Day on November seventeenth, nineteen ninety-nine, to promote the use of many different languages around the world. The celebration happens every year on February twenty-first. This date was chosen to remember the day in nineteen fifty-two in Bangladesh when students died while protesting for Bengali to be recognized as a national language of Pakistan. UNESCO picks a different theme each year for the celebration to promote different parts of language education. Twenty twenty-five marks the twenty-fifth anniversary of International Mother Language Day, which reminds us how important it is to have many different languages to help create dignity, peace, and understanding. According to UNESCO's Atlas of the World's Languages in Danger, there are over eight thousand languages that exist in the world. About one thousand one hundred eighty-one of these languages are no longer being used, and about six thousand languages are classified as endangered, which means they could disappear within a few generations. The United States Census Bureau has found that over three hundred fifty languages are spoken in the United States. At least one hundred sixty of these are distinct Native North American languages. According to the UNESCO Atlas, two hundred eighty Native North American languages were spoken in the continental United States when Europeans first arrived. More than one hundred fifteen of these languages are already extinct, and many more are critically endangered. The United States has a rich and diverse cultural heritage, and preserving existing languages is an essential part of preserving this heritage.

sections.title: What the House of Representatives Decides
sections.text: The House of Representatives supports the goals and ideals of International Mother Language Day. The House also encourages the people of the United States to observe International Mother Language Day with appropriate ceremonies, programs, and activities.
